UKRI Mental Health Platform

2,990 followers

1mo

Taking Action at the MHP🗣️ This year for #MentalHealthAwareness week, we are putting a spotlight on researchers and teams at the Mental Health Platform who are taking Action to improve approaches in the treatment, diagnosis, and management of severe mental illness. Read the blog from our Cross-Hub ECR Fellow, Dr Samuel Leighton 👉 read here https://lnkd.in/ep_QVADF 🖥️ Bridging across ImmunoMIND and the Hub for Metabolic Psychiatry, Sam is using machine learning to better predict physical health outcomes that may accompany diagnoses of psychosis. This work may enable the implementation of more effective personalised medicine. Restoring agency and the ability to take action for people living with SMI.

    Our Western mental health umbrella's foundation is like going to McDonald’s, thinking you are going to get a nutritious meal. 🍌 Because the foundational pillars are all based on psychiatry, which is the least scientific field of them all.. 💡 We do indeed have amazing neuroscience since over 25 years back, this has, however, not fully trickled into clinical practice, meaning so often the very umbrella fails to serve its purpose, and so often creates further harm, or is simply inept. The science is there for a massive paradigm shift, one where we would fully acknowledge childhood trauma, such as abuse and neglect, and its impact, and finally meet it with competence. One where neuroscience and developmental trauma are the very pillars of foundational training for therapists. I hope I don’t die young because there is A LOT of work to do… Rock’n’roll, All my Love, Nic #CPTSD #CPTSDTech #ComplexTrauma #ChildhoodTrauma

    Feeling heard without being “fixed” can be deeply regulating in itself. Slowing down enough to listen creates the kind of safety that allows people to actually process what they’re carrying. It’s a simple shift, but it can have a meaningful impact in both clinical work and everyday relationships. #TraumaHealing #TraumaInformed

        Abreacted refers to the release or expression of previously suppressed emotional tension, trauma, or conflict; often through intense emotional reaction, recollection, speech, crying, anger, or cathartic behaviour. In psychology and psychiatry, abreaction describes a process where hidden or repressed emotions are brought into conscious awareness and emotionally discharged. Example: “During therapy, the patient abreacted years of unresolved grief.”

      • Qazi Javed
        1mo
        • Report this post

        "It doesn't make sense" is something I hear too often, not from the mind, but from the heart. A patient may enter my office reporting anxiety or depression, but the underlying issue is frequently an absence of meaning in their life. In many cases, they have achieved societal markers of success yet feel hollow or disoriented. Traditional psychiatry can offer symptom relief, but this often feels like treating the leaves of a tree while ignoring its roots. One cannot overlook Viktor Frankl's existential vacuum; it's a concept continually affirmed in sessions where patients articulate a sense of being unmoored despite stable life circumstances. The human brain is wired to find patterns and purpose, possibly as an evolutionary necessity for making sense of our complex world. When that wiring is disrupted—whether by trauma, life transitions, or spiritual crises—the resulting disharmony manifests as psychiatric symptoms. Neuroimaging studies suggest shifts in the prefrontal cortex may underlie this, but a scan isn't what's missing for these patients—it's a dialogue about meaning. Integrative approaches, like those offered by contemplative traditions or plant-based therapies backed by emerging research, sometimes create spaces where patients can start to explore these deeper issues. How do we create structures within modern psychiatry to honor this search for meaning without straying into the nebulous? Can we, as clinicians, hold room for this without letting go of scientific rigor? #IntegrativePsychiatry #MeaningAndMind #DepthPsychology

        🌍 Europe is undergoing profound social, political, and technological transformation, with direct implications for mental health and the systems designed to support it. As demand rises and pressures intensify, psychiatry is being called to respond at scale, while maintaining quality, equity, and continuity of care. 🤝 Against this backdrop, this year’s European Mental Health Awareness Week theme - “Stronger Together: Prioritise Mental Health in a Changing Europe” - highlights both the urgency of the challenge and the need for collective action. 📝 In this thought-leadership paper, “Stronger Together: The Future of Psychiatry in a Changing Europe”, EPA President Prof. Andrea Fiorillo and EPA President-Elect Prof. Celso Arango share how collaboration is essential in the future of psychiatry in Europe, and how implementing personalised and integrated care means recognising the uniqueness of each individual through a “Whole Person Health” approach. 🔗👉 Read the article: https://lnkd.in/dpKWznyx

        In this episode of Collegial Conversations, host Diana Clark, JD,MA speaks with Dr. Christopher Palmer, a Harvard psychiatrist and leading voice in metabolic psychiatry. Dr. Palmer shares how his work is reshaping the way we think about psychiatric conditions—through the lens of brain metabolism. From powerful patient stories to emerging research, this conversation explores a new frontier in mental health care. 🔹 What is metabolic psychiatry?  🔹 How ketogenic therapy is being used in clinical settings  🔹 Why some people don’t respond to traditional treatments  🔹 What recovery can really look like You can watch this compelling episode here: https://lnkd.in/etR-Tpsi You can learn more about Dr. Palmer’s work, including MH2 and his research on brain energy, at: https://lnkd.in/ewa4DV2p  https://mh2.health  https://brainenergy.com/ #MentalHealth #BrainHealth #MetabolicPsychiatry #MetabolicHealth #MentalHealthEducation #MindBodyScience #WholePersonHealth

      • Diana Clark, JD,MA
        1mo
        • Report this post

        Could the way we fuel the brain be the missing piece in mental health care? I recently spoke with Dr. Christopher Palmer about his work at the intersection of metabolism and psychiatry and how it’s reshaping his approach to treatment. What stood out most wasn’t just the theory. It was the clinical reality: patients who weren’t improving with standardized care, and the question that kept coming up—what else is going on here? For a long time, the focus has been on managing symptoms. This conversation pushes a step deeper, toward the underlying biology of how the brain functions. It’s not a simple answer. But it’s a meaningful shift in how we think healing is possible.  #MentalHealth #BrainHealth #MetabolicPsychiatry #MetabolicHealth #MentalHealthEducation #MindBodyScience #WholePersonHealth
